response: The internet originated from 1960s U.S. Department of Defense-funded computer networking research, with its precursor ARPANET first demonstrated in 1969 via a UCLA-to-Stanford Research Institute message; critical foundational leaps included 1983’s TCP/IP protocol transition (laying the modern internet’s technical groundwork) and Tim Berners-Lee’s 1989 World Wide Web invention at CERN—introducing core tools like HTML, URLs, and HTTP—followed by 1993’s Mosaic browser, which opened the web to non-research users. Subsequent decades brought pivotal commercial and societal shifts: the late 1990s dot-com boom, which saw massive internet company investment followed by a 2001 crash that spawned enduring industry giants like Amazon and Google; the mid-2000s rise of social media platforms (Facebook, Twitter, YouTube) that redefined communication; 2006’s launch of Amazon Web Services (AWS) establishing cloud computing as a major tech force; and 2007’s iPhone debut accelerating the spread of ubiquitous mobile internet. Today, the internet is a global foundational infrastructure connecting over 5 billion people, underpinning nearly every facet of modern life, including commerce, communication, and entertainment.
